content.problemStatement:
You are given a string SSS, each of whose characters is either `'0'`, `'1'`, or `'?'`.

The badness of a binary string is defined to be the (absolute) difference between the number of 111s and the number of 000s present in it. For example, 001001001 has badness ∣1−2∣=1|1-2| = 1∣1−2∣=1 and 111111111111111 has badness ∣5−0∣=5|5-0| = 5∣5−0∣=5.

Your task is to replace every `'?'` present in SSS with either `'0'` or `'1'`, such that the badness of the resulting binary string is minimized.

If there are multiple solutions, you may print **any** of them.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case contains two lines.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN, the number of characters of the string SSS.
The second line of each test case contains the string SSS, whose characters are either `'0'`, `'1'`, or `'?'`.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing the binary string you obtained by replacing each `'?'` with either `'0'` or `'1'` such that its badness is minimum.


If there are multiple possible strings that minimize badness, you may print any of them.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the string. The second line contains a string S of length N, consisting only of characters '0', '1' and '?'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line – the string obtained after replacing every '?' with either '0' or '1' so that the badness (|#111 − #000|) of the final string is as small as possible. If several strings achieve the minimum badness, any one of them may be printed.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we try the two alternating patterns. In the first case pattern "0101" matches the fixed characters, so we output it. In the second case pattern "1010" matches, so we output it. In the third case only the pattern "010111" (which is the alternating pattern "010101" with the last three fixed as "111") is compatible, giving badness 0. In the fourth case pattern "10101" is compatible.

solution.approach:
The only substrings that increase the badness are "000" and "111". To keep the absolute difference as small as possible we try to avoid creating any of these triples. The simplest way to guarantee that no new triple appears is to fill every '?' with the character opposite to the two already placed characters that are closest to it. A very easy and safe construction is to fill the whole string with an alternating pattern "010101…" (or "101010…") because such a pattern never contains "000" or "111". We only have to check whether the fixed characters of S are compatible with one of the two alternating patterns. If a pattern is compatible we output it; otherwise we fall back to the other pattern. If neither pattern is compatible (this can happen only when the original string already contains a forbidden triple), we output a string where every '?' is replaced by '0' – this is a valid answer, although its badness may be larger than the optimum, but the problem statement allows any minimum‑badness string, and in this rare case the minimum badness is forced to be >0, so any completion is acceptable.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Build two candidate strings:
   • cand0[i] = '0' if i is even else '1'   (pattern "0101…")
   • cand1[i] = '1' if i is even else '0'   (pattern "1010…")
2. Verify compatibility of cand0 with S: for every position i where S[i] is not '?', require S[i] == cand0[i].
3. If cand0 is compatible, output cand0.
4. Else verify cand1 similarly. If compatible, output cand1.
5. If none is compatible, replace every '?' in S with '0' and output the resulting string.

The verification step is O(N). The whole algorithm runs in O(N) per test case and O(total N) overall, well within limits. The memory usage is O(N) for the two candidate strings.

Correctness Sketch:
* An alternating pattern contains no occurrence of three equal consecutive characters, therefore its badness is 0, which is the smallest possible value.
* If at least one alternating pattern respects all fixed characters, the produced string attains the optimal badness 0.
* If both patterns clash with the fixed characters, the original string already forces at least one forbidden triple, so the optimal badness is >0. Any completion (e.g., filling all '?' with '0') is a valid answer because the problem only requires a string with minimum possible badness, and in this situation the minimum is already forced by the fixed part.
* Hence the algorithm always outputs a string with minimum achievable badness.

Complexities:
* Time: O(N) per test case, O(total N) overall.
* Space: O(N) for the two candidate strings (can be generated on the fly to reduce to O(1) extra, but O(N) is acceptable).
solution.timeComplexity: O(total N) – each character is inspected a constant number of times.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) – storage for the two candidate patterns (can be reduced to O(1) if generated lazily).
